output.title: Landlord Withholding Your Security Deposit? Here's How to Fight Back and Win
output.meta_description: Is your landlord withholding your security deposit? Learn your tenant rights, what landlords can deduct, and how to send a demand letter that gets your money back.
content_outline: Why Landlords Withhold Security Deposits (And Why Most Excuses Are Illegal)
Tenant Rights: How Long Does a Landlord Have to Return Your Deposit?
What Can a Landlord Deduct from Your Security Deposit vs. Normal Wear and Tear
What to Do When Your Landlord Doesn't Return Your Deposit: A Step-by-Step Guide
How to Write a Security Deposit Dispute Letter That Actually Gets Results
key_points: Most states require landlords to return security deposits within 14–60 days and provide an itemized deduction list
Normal wear and tear, routine cleaning, and minor scuffs cannot be deducted from your deposit in most jurisdictions
A formal demand letter is legally required before small claims court in many states and often forces immediate payment
DepositBack generates state-specific, legally compliant security deposit demand letters in under 5 minutes
output.cta: Stop waiting. Generate your state-specific security deposit demand letter with DepositBack in minutes and force your landlord to respond.
output.target_audience: Frustrated US renters who have moved out and are dealing with a landlord not returning their security deposit or making unlawful deductions

email_sequence.subject: Your landlord is withholding your security deposit. Now what?
email_sequence.body:
Hi [First Name],

Moving is stressful enough without your landlord keeping money that belongs to you. If your landlord is withholding your security deposit—or sent a vague list of deductions—you're not powerless.

Every state has strict deadlines. In many places, if your security deposit is not returned within 30 days with an itemized statement, your landlord forfeits the right to keep a dime.

Tomorrow, I'll show you exactly what your landlord can and cannot deduct. Spoiler: "cleaning" and "fresh paint" usually aren't valid reasons.

Stay tuned,
[Name]

email_sequence.subject: What can a landlord deduct from your security deposit? (Hint: Not "cleaning")
email_sequence.body:
Hi [First Name],

Let's talk about what your landlord can actually deduct from your security deposit.

Legally? Unpaid rent and damage beyond normal wear and tear. That's it.

Carpet worn from walking? Not deductible. Small nail holes? Not deductible. General cleaning? Not deductible.

If your landlord won't give you your deposit back for these "reasons," they're likely violating state law. Many tenants don't know this—and landlords count on your silence.

Knowledge is power, but action gets checks cashed. I'll show you the exact next step tomorrow.

[Name]

email_sequence.subject: The security deposit dispute letter that makes landlords pay attention
email_sequence.body:
Hi [First Name],

You've asked nicely. You've waited. Still no deposit.

It's time to escalate with a security deposit dispute letter that cites your state's specific landlord-tenant statutes.

Why? Because a properly written demand letter triggers legal deadlines. It shows you know your rights. And in most states, it is a prerequisite for suing—and winning—double or triple damages.

But writing one yourself is risky. Wrong format? Wrong statute? Your landlord ignores it.

That's why I built DepositBack. We generate a state-specific, legally compliant demand letter in minutes using the same language attorneys use.

[CTA Link]

The longer you wait, the harder your case gets. Send your letter today.

[Name]

Don't let anxiety or procrastination cost you hundreds—or thousands—of dollars.
